column1: # Function-style def: bind a name to a code body, then invoke.
# `def NAME [body]` registers NAME → list-body in the def stack.
# Subsequent occurrences of NAME splice the body's elements at the
# pointer so they execute inline against the current stack.
# Spec subset semantics: lists bound via `def` are always treated as
# code bodies — there's no Quoted/Eval distinction here. The full
# Go engine uses an Eval flag and a `quote` word to discriminate
# data lists from code bodies; both are listed in the engine.ts
# PARITY GAP and would need separate ports to support `def x [data]`
# as data.
# Test word:
#   def — sig [Word, Any]. Captures the name from a Word token,
#         binds it to the value in the def stack. Returns [].
# tokens
def double [ dup add ] 5 double
column3: body executes against stack=[5]: dup→[5,5], add→[10]

column1: def square [ dup mul ] 7 square
def inc [ 1 add ] 5 inc
column3: body adds 1 to top of stack

column1: def add5 [ add 5 ] 10 add5
column3: curried form — body uses an embedded constant

column1: def double [ dup add ] ( 3 double ) ( 5 double )
column3: parens isolate each invocation; without them the inner add would greedily grab the second arg

column1: def double [ dup add ] def quad [ double double ] 3 quad
def pi 3 pi add 1
column3: bare scalar def still substitutes (existing simple-value path)
